parents.father_research_notes: Son of Rev. John Wilson Sr., first minister First Church Boston

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Medfield church — served forty years
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first minister of Medfield, Massachusetts in 1651 and served the congregation for forty years until his death in 1691. He had previously served as a colleague of Rev. Richard Mather at Dorchester, and received his education at Harvard College in 1642.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard 1642 — the first graduating class
narrative_blocks.body: He attended Harvard College in 1642, effectively the first graduating class of the institution. His father Rev. John Wilson Sr. was a close ally of Rev. Thomas Hooker, and John Jr. married Hooker's daughter Sarah — bringing together two of the founding families of Puritan New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Boston's founding minister
narrative_blocks.body: His father, Rev. John Wilson Sr., was the first minister of the First Church of Boston from its founding in 1630 until his death in 1667 — the man who formally excommunicated Anne Hutchinson in 1638 and who supervised the execution of Mary Dyer in 1660.

narrative_blocks.header: Fell four stories — taken up for dead
narrative_blocks.body: Cotton Mather records that as a child, Wilson "fell upon his head from a loft four stories high into the street; from whence he was taken up for dead, and so battered and bruised and bloody with his fall, that it struck horror into the beholders." His father's prayers were credited with his recovery. He went on to serve Medfield forty years as minister, physician, and schoolmaster.

career.notes: Studied at Eaton's school (introductory to Harvard). Testified to ill-treatment of scholars by Eaton.
Colleague of Rev. Richard Mather at Dorchester. Known as "the gracious and godly Mr. Wilson."
Settled 1651. Ordained pastor Oct 12, 1652. Also served as physician and schoolmaster. Served 40 years until his death.

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Rev. John Wilson Sr., first minister First Church Boston

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Medfield church — served forty years
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first minister of Medfield, Massachusetts in 1651 and served the congregation for forty years until his death in 1691. He had previously served as a colleague of Rev. Richard Mather at Dorchester, and received his education at Harvard College in 1642.

Thomas Hooker, and John Jr. married Hooker's daughter Sarah — bringing together two of the founding families of Puritan New England.

narrative_blocks.body: His father, Rev. John Wilson Sr., was the first minister of the First Church of Boston from its founding in 1630 until his death in 1667 — the man who formally excommunicated Anne Hutchinson in 1638 and who supervised the execution of Mary Dyer in 1660.

His father's prayers were credited with his recovery. He went on to serve Medfield forty years as minister, physician, and schoolmaster.

Colleague of Rev. Richard Mather at Dorchester. Known as "the gracious and godly Mr. Wilson."
Settled 1651. Ordained pastor Oct 12, 1652. Also served as physician and schoolmaster. Served 40 years until his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to age 95
narrative_blocks.body: Lived to age 95

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived husband by 34 years
narrative_blocks.body: Outlived husband by 34 years

narrative_blocks.header: Her son married her sister Mary's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Her son John Wilson married Sarah Newton — the daughter of Mary Hooker Newton, Sarah's own sister. Both of Thomas Hooker's daughters had children of the same generation who married each other, making their grandchildren the first documented double-descent in the Hooker tree.

cross_connections.display_label: Her son John Wilson married his first cousin Sarah Newton.
was her father-in-law and Boston’s first minister

narrative_blocks.header: John died young
narrative_blocks.body: John died in 1659 at the age of nine. John’s parents gave the name to a child born one year later.

cross_connections.display_label: Her daughter Sarah Batt married Josiah's nephew.
Her daughter Sarah Batt married Josiah's nephew.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost first husband Paul Batt within four years
narrative_blocks.body: She married Paul Batt in 1674; he died in Boston in July 1678, leaving her widowed at twenty-eight. Two years later she married Josiah Torrey of Medfield and bore him three more children.

narrative_blocks.header: Three colonies across two marriages
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Dorchester and raised in Boston, she moved to Medfield with second husband Josiah Torrey, then on to Mansfield, Connecticut. She died in Mansfield around 1725, having lived in three of the New England colonies during her seventy-five years.

bio.bio_blurb: Salisbury-born twin; Sarah Wilson’s first husband

narrative_blocks.header: He came of Salisbury’s tanner-grantee family
narrative_blocks.body: Paul Batt was born a twin at Salisbury in 1643, into the family of Christopher Batt — a tanner and one of the town’s twelve original grantees, who had crossed from Wiltshire on the ship Bevis in 1638, slipping out just ahead of the King’s ban on sailings.

narrative_blocks.header: His father died in a freak shooting
narrative_blocks.body: When Paul was a young man his father was accidentally shot and killed by another of his sons, who had been firing at a target in the family orchard at Boston.

narrative_blocks.header: He left his shop to his daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Paul married Sarah Wilson in 1674 and died in 1678 at about thirty-five; his will left his Boston tenement and shop to his infant daughter Sarah, charged with the support of his widowed mother.

cross_connections.display_label: His stepdaughter Sarah Batt married his own nephew.

career.notes: A man of influence in every town he settled — Weymouth, Boston, Mendon, Bristol, and Mansfield.
Rose from lieutenant to captain of the local trainband.
Served many years as a deputy and clerk of court.

bio.title: Rev. Capt.
bio.bio_blurb: Clothier and militia captain of colonial New England

narrative_blocks.header: A clothier whose influence followed every move
narrative_blocks.body: Josiah Torrey worked as a clothier and clothweaver and was remembered as a man of influence in each of the towns he lived in, from Weymouth and Boston to Mansfield, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the minister’s widowed daughter
narrative_blocks.body: In 1680 Josiah married Sarah Wilson — daughter of Rev. John Wilson Jr. and Sarah Hooker, and widow of the Boston merchant Paul Batt.

cross_connections.display_label: Sarah Batt married Micajah Torrey Jr. — whose father Micajah.
Sarah Batt married Micajah Torrey Jr. — whose father Micajah.
Sarah Batt married Micajah Torrey Jr. — whose father Micajah.

open_questions: Torrey line not fully researched beyond Weymouth — further descendants possible. See open_research_threads.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her step-uncle's son
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Batt's stepfather Josiah Torrey was the brother of her husband Micajah Torrey Jr.'s father Micajah Torrey Sr. — Sarah therefore married her step-uncle's son. Her own children were both step-cousins to and children-in-law of the broader Torrey family network. The step-family marriage pattern was common in colonial New England where remarriages frequently rearranged the kinship of small village populations.

narrative_blocks.header: Nine documented children with Micajah Torrey Jr.
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah and Micajah Torrey Jr. had nine documented children. The marriage produced the largest single Batt-Torrey family cluster of the late-seventeenth and early-eighteenth-century Boston-and-Weymouth Massachusetts area, with the family settling primarily at Weymouth where Sarah lived out her long widowhood-and-old-age decades.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to ninety; gravestone reads eighty-fifth year
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah lived to approximately age ninety — born January 18, 1673 in Boston and died May 10, 1763 in Weymouth, a span of ninety years and three months. The Weymouth gravestone reads 'in ye 85th year of her age' — a discrepancy suggesting either an inaccurate stone inscription or a contemporary error in computing her age. The colonial gravestone tradition of counting the running year rather than completed years often produced such variations.

narrative_blocks.header: Boston 1673 to Weymouth Massachusetts 1763
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Batt was born January 18, 1673 in Boston Massachusetts, daughter of Paul Batt and Sarah Wilson Torrey. She died May 10, 1763 in Weymouth Massachusetts. Her ninety-year life spanned from the early years of King Philip's War through to the eve of the American Revolution.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed twice, she married a deacon
narrative_blocks.body: Three years after Micajah Torrey died, Sarah married Deacon Samuel White of Braintree, becoming the second wife of a man who outlived her first husband by decades.

bio.bio_blurb: Lost at sea in a 1703 snowstorm

narrative_blocks.header: He was lost at sea in a snowstorm
narrative_blocks.body: Paul married Elizabeth Mighill at Boston early in 1701 and was lost at sea in a snowstorm on 28 November 1703, less than three years later, at about twenty-eight.

bio.title: Rev.
bio.bio_blurb: Minister at Tisbury on Martha’s Vineyard

narrative_blocks.header: The son who became Tisbury’s minister
narrative_blocks.body: Where his father was a clothier and militia captain, Josiah Torrey Jr. took the pulpit, serving as minister at Tisbury on Martha’s Vineyard until his death at forty-two.

narrative_blocks.header: Three founding ministers — father, grandfather, great-grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Rev. John Wilson Jr. was the first minister of Medfield. His father Rev. John Wilson Sr. was the first minister of the First Church of Boston. Her maternal grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker was the first minister of Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: First minister of Dunstable — died age 48
narrative_blocks.body: He preached in Dunstable from May 1, 1679, gathered a church, and was ordained December 16, 1685. A 1702 diary entry records: "Mr. Thomas Weld, the pastor of the Church of Dunstable was buryed — he was an eminent preacher of the word of god, a man well beloued and much Lamented by them that knew him." He died June 9, 1702, age 48.

narrative_blocks.header: King William's War emptied two thirds of Dunstable
narrative_blocks.body: From 1691, Indian hostilities during King William's War (1689-1697) so alarmed his Dunstable congregation that "near two thirds of the inhabitants moved away." Weld preached through years of frontier danger, died June 7, 1702, at the very beginning of Queen Anne's War — Dunstable was among the most exposed settlements in Massachusetts.

burial.plot_notes: Old Dunstable Cemetery (also called Little Cemetery / Littles Station Cemetery), now Nashua, Hillsborough County, New Hampshire. FindAGrave confirms.

career.notes: Represented Deerfield at session beginning May 23, 1689, after deposition of Sir Edmund Andros.

Began preaching 1679; ordained Dec 16, 1685. 5th church organized within limits of NH. Ministry disrupted by King William's War (Indian hostilities beginning 1691); near two thirds of inhabitants moved away. Died at beginning of Queen Anne's War, June 7, 1702.

narrative_blocks.header: Elizabeth died young
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth died young; the year was not recorded.

cross_connections.notes: Their children carry descent from Thomas Hooker through two separate lines — descent_paths_to_thomas: 2
cross_connections.display_label: John Wilson and his wife Sarah Newton were first cousins.

cross_connections.notes: Their children carry descent from Thomas Hooker through two separate lines — descent_paths_to_thomas: 2
cross_connections.display_label: John Wilson and his wife Sarah Newton were first cousins.

cross_connections.notes: Their children carry descent from Thomas Hooker through two separate lines — descent_paths_to_thomas: 2
cross_connections.display_label: John Wilson and his wife Sarah Newton were first cousins.

institutions.institution_blurb: Left Harvard College soon after admission; educated by his father instead.

narrative_blocks.header: A regicide praised his preaching in 1682
narrative_blocks.body: In 1682 John Wilson preached in New Haven for over a year. Col. John Dixwell — one of the English regicides then living in hiding in New Haven — wrote to Rev. Increase Mather calling him "a veire hopefull yonge man of good abilities." Wilson returned to Medfield in 1684 and eventually abandoned the ministry.

narrative_blocks.header: Left Harvard — his father taught him everything
narrative_blocks.body: He left Harvard College soon after admission, but received a thorough private education from his father in divinity, medicine, and law. He settled in Braintree in 1692 and spent the rest of his life farming, practicing medicine, and performing legal services.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin — both Hooker grandchildren
narrative_blocks.body: His mother was Sarah Hooker Wilson (Thomas Hooker's daughter); his wife Sarah Newton's mother was Mary Hooker Newton (also Thomas Hooker's daughter). The two mothers were sisters. EH and the Wilson genealogy both explicitly note the cousin relationship.

career.notes: Employed as schoolmaster in Medfield for several terms; also assisted father in ministry.

military_service.notes: Commission as captain of one of the local military companies, 1696.

cross_connections.display_label: She and her first cousin John Wilson married — Hooker descendants.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin — both Hooker grandchildren
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband John Wilson was her first cousin: her mother was Mary Hooker, his mother was Sarah Hooker — sisters, both daughters of Rev. Thomas Hooker. They married July 4, 1683 in Milford. EH and the Wilson family genealogy both confirm the cousin relationship explicitly.

narrative_blocks.header: Married 27 days after her father's death
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Roger Newton Sr. died June 7, 1683. She married John Wilson on July 4, 1683 — twenty-seven days later. The marriage had presumably been planned before his death, but she wed her cousin in the immediate shadow of her father's loss.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited 150 Farmington acres from her father
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Roger Newton Sr.'s 1683 will left her 150 acres in 'Bohemia' Farmington. Family tradition speculates the land may have originally been given to her mother Mary Hooker by Governor Haynes — connecting it to the Hartford founding generation.

narrative_blocks.header: First with Hooker descent through both parents
narrative_blocks.body: She was the first generation in this tree where both parents descended from Thomas Hooker. Her father came through Sarah Hooker's marriage to Rev. John Wilson Jr.; her mother through Mary Hooker's marriage to Rev. Roger Newton.

narrative_blocks.header: Named her son Wilson to mark the lineage
narrative_blocks.body: Among her twelve children with John Marsh, she named one Wilson Marsh — honoring her Wilson and Hooker ancestors four generations after Thomas Hooker. The pattern parallels other Hooker-descendant naming conventions of the era.

sources.notes: EH gives only birth year, Harvard graduation, death year — no children recorded

ministry_positions.notes: Preaching at what became the town of Barrington when he died Nov 23, 1713

estate_accounts.notes: Debts received, debts paid, provisions for family, administration fees

estate_accounts.by: widow Margaret Terry, wife of Samuel Terry
estate_accounts.notes: Requested personal items to support posthumous child Margaret
Sale of land, allowance to widow, items sold at a loss, administration fees

sources.notes: EH gives only birth year and spouse surname Low

narrative_blocks.header: Named her son Hooker — four generations on
narrative_blocks.body: Among her four children with Samuel Low was a son named Hooker Low — an explicit tribute to her Hooker ancestry four generations on. Her grandmother was Mary Hooker Newton, daughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker. The name Hooker as a given name is a deliberate act of family memory.

sources.notes: Children list with detailed birth dates

burial.inscription: Here lies Inter'd ye Remains of Mrs. Susanna Rawson Relict of ye late Rev'd Mr. Grindall Rawson, Pastor of a Church of Christ in Mendon & Daughter of ye Rev'd Mr. John Wilson 1st Minister of ye Gospel in Medfield who changed mortality for life Aug. 23, 1691. She was born Dec. 1, 1664, & died July 8, 1748, in ye 84th year of her age.

sources.full: Gary Boyd Roberts, Ancestors of American Presidents
sources.label: Roberts 2009

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote Cotton Mather a eulogy for her husband
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Grindal Rawson died on February 6, 1715, Susanna wrote a long letter to Cotton Mather — a detailed portrait of her husband's forty-year ministry at Mendon, his Indian mission work, his character, and his final hours. His last words were "Come Lord Jesus, come quickly!" The letter was published in the Rawson Family Memorial. Cotton Mather quoted it in the sermon he preached at Rawson's funeral.

narrative_blocks.header: Her gravestone names her father, not husband
narrative_blocks.body: Her gravestone in Mendon reads: "Here lies inter'd ye Remains of Mrs. Susanna Rawson, Relict of ye late Rev. Mr. Grindal Rawson... and daughter to ye Rev. Mr. John Wilson 1st Minister of ye Gospel in Medfield." She outlived her husband by 33 years, dying July 8, 1748, age 83.

narrative_blocks.header: Ancestor of President Taft through both Taft brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Her granddaughter Mary Dorr Taft married Moses Taft, and her great-granddaughter Rhoda Rawson Taft married Aaron Taft — Moses's and Aaron's fathers were brothers. President William Howard Taft descends through the Aaron Taft line.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, the Bay Colony’s secretary

notable.notable_blurb: Mendon minister; Algonquian translator; Cotton Mather classmate

quotes.text: To my Beloved Wife Mrs Susanna Rawson who tho last in mention yet deserveth the first in my best and sincere affections
quotes.attribution: Rev. Grindal Rawson, Will dated Jan 24, 1714

narrative_blocks.header: Named for Elizabeth I's Archbishop of Canterbury
narrative_blocks.body: He was named "Grindal" after Edmund Grindal, Archbishop of Canterbury under Elizabeth I — his great-great-grandmother's brother, per the Harvard commencement of 1678. At that ceremony President Urian Oakes called him "sprung from a most illustrious stock" and prayed that Rawson might "in learning, holiness and excellence of character, resemble both Wilson and Grindal."

narrative_blocks.header: Mastered Algonquian; preached and translated for natives
narrative_blocks.body: He learned the Algonquian language and preached sermons in it to local Nipmuc parishioners — translating Thomas Shepard's Sincere Convert, John Cotton's Spiritual Milk, and a Confession of Faith. Cotton Mather called him 'a Master that had scarce an Equal' in the language.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to sail with the Canada invasion
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1692 the General Court ordered four ministers, Rawson among them, to accompany the forces in the expedition against Canada, “to carry on the worshipping of God.” Whether he sailed with Sir William Phipps is unknown.

cross_connections.display_label: carried the Mendon pastor’s name into the Talcott line

bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Gravestone portrait, Mendon burying ground — primary for authenticity.

narrative_blocks.header: His two sons each married an Allen sister
narrative_blocks.body: Both his sons Edmund and Abner married daughters of the same Allen family of Medway — Martha Allen and Mary Allen respectively. He was Deacon Edmund Rawson of Uxbridge; his sons' double Allen marriage kept the two families closely bound across a generation.

narrative_blocks.header: She shared his birthday, died on her anniversary
narrative_blocks.body: Born June 22 like her husband, nine years apart, Mary Rawson Dorr died on April 9, 1776 — fifty-two years to the day after her wedding. Her stone reads “Dust thou art and unto dust shalt thou return.”

cross_connections.display_label: grew up in Grindall’s Hadlyme congregation, later its deacon

narrative_blocks.header: South Hadley’s first settled minister
narrative_blocks.body: Grindall followed his father into the pulpit — Harvard 1728, then nine years as South Hadley’s first settled minister before his 1745 introduction to the Hadlyme parish, partly in Lyme and partly in East Haddam, where he remained for life.

parents.father_research_notes: Chaplain to Archbishop of Canterbury Edmund Grindal; prebend of St Paul's, London.

institutions.institution_blurb: First minister of First Church Boston from its founding in 1630 until his death in 1667.

notable.notable_blurb: First minister of Boston; excommunicated Anne Hutchinson

cross_connections.display_label: Both prosecuted Hutchinson — Shepard's voice, Wilson's verdict.
was Hooker’s daughter and married his son John

narrative_blocks.header: He excommunicated Anne Hutchinson from the pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: At the conclusion of her church trial in March 1638, Wilson delivered the final blow: "Forasmuch as you, Mrs. Hutchinson, have highly transgressed and offended...I doe cast you out and deliver you up to Sathan...I command you in the name of Christ Jesus...as a Leper to withdraw your selfe out of the Congregation." Hutchinson left the colony within a week.

narrative_blocks.header: Mary Dyer: nay, I repent not
narrative_blocks.body: On June 1, 1660, Wilson watched his former parishioner Mary Dyer approach the Boston gallows for the second time after returning to the colony against orders. He called to her: "Mary Dyer, O repent, O repent, and be not so deluded and carried away by deceit of the devil." She replied: "Nay, man, I am not now to repent." The ladder was kicked away.

narrative_blocks.header: His son married Thomas Hooker's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: His son John Wilson Jr. attended Harvard in 1642 and married Sarah Hooker, daughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford. The two men likely formed a friendship at Emmanuel College, Cambridge, years before either came to New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Called himself vile — refused any portrait
narrative_blocks.body: When urged to sit for a portrait, Wilson replied: "What! Such a poor vile creature as I am! Shall my picture be drawn?

quotes.text: I doe cast you out and...deliver you up to Sathan...I command you in the name of Ch[rist] Je[sus] and of this Church as a Leper to withdraw your selfe out of the Congregation.
quotes.attribution: Rev. John Wilson Sr., excommunicating Anne Hutchinson, March 1638

career.notes: Chaplain to the Countess of Leicester and other prominent families.
Suspended and reinstated several times for Puritan nonconformity.
First minister of the settlers at Charlestown, then Boston. Installed teacher Aug 27, 1630. Served until his death.

education.notes: Four years; chosen to speak Latin oration before duc de Biron, ambassador from Henry IV of France.
M.A. 1613. Noted for Puritan advocacy. Likely formed friendship with Thomas Hooker here.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Line drawing, Massachusetts Historical Society; said to have been made after his death as Wilson refused to sit for a portrait.

narrative_blocks.header: She refused to cross to New England
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Mansfield long resisted following her husband to Massachusetts; Margaret Winthrop’s 1631 letters marveled that she was “more averse than ever she was” before she finally sailed in 1632.

burialCemetery.name: Vine Lake Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Vine Lake Cemetery, Medfield, Norfolk County, Massachusetts.

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.
